key_insight: PI-Splines replace the neural network in physics-informed learning with a trainable tensor-product B-spline field, keeping residual-based training while adding locality, smoothness control, and interpretable parameters.
engineering_relevance: This is useful for PDE-constrained modeling when compact support, stable derivatives, and strong boundary enforcement matter more than a fully flexible black-box network.
method: Represent the unknown solution as a tensor-product B-spline expansion with trainable control coefficients.
Train the spline coefficients by minimizing the physics residual, like in PINNs.
Impose boundary conditions strongly by fixing boundary control points when the spline basis allows it.
evidence_signal: The paper benchmarks PI-Splines on several PDE test problems and compares them against standard physics-informed methods under matched equations, collocation points, losses, and optimization settings.
watch_item: The approach depends on choosing a spline representation compatible with the problem geometry and boundary conditions, so it may be less straightforward to apply in highly irregular domains.
paper_title: Trainable Spline Representations for Physics-Informed Learning
